title: National Income & Macroeconomics
description: GDP measurement, national income accounting, consumption and saving functions, and aggregate demand and supply

questions.statement: Microeconomics studies the economy as a whole, including GDP and inflation.
questions.payload: FALSE. Microeconomics studies individual units like households and firms; macro studies the whole economy.

questions.statement: GDP measures the market value of all final goods and services produced within a country's borders.
questions.payload: TRUE. GDP counts output produced within the country's borders regardless of who produces it.

questions.statement: Remittances sent by Ethiopians working in Dubai are included in Ethiopia's GDP.
questions.payload: FALSE. Remittances are included in GNP, which counts nationals wherever they are, not in GDP.

questions.statement: In a closed economy with no foreign sector, GNP equals GDP.
questions.payload: TRUE. With no cross-border factor income, there is no difference between GNP and GDP.

questions.statement: The expenditure approach and income approach to measuring GDP give different conceptual results.
questions.payload: FALSE. All three approaches measure the same thing and should give the same result in theory.

questions.statement: GDP by the expenditure approach equals C + I + G + (X - M).
questions.payload: TRUE. This sums consumption, investment, government spending, and net exports.

questions.statement: Nominal GDP is a better measure of real output growth than real GDP.
questions.payload: FALSE. Nominal GDP can rise from price increases alone; real GDP removes inflation.

questions.statement: Real GDP is measured using base-year prices to remove the effect of inflation.
questions.payload: TRUE. Real GDP uses constant prices to show the true change in output volume.

questions.statement: The GDP deflator is calculated as (Nominal GDP / Real GDP) x 100.
questions.payload: TRUE. The deflator is an implicit price index measuring overall price level changes.

questions.statement: MPC + MPS = 1 is always true by definition.
questions.payload: TRUE. Any extra income must be either consumed or saved, so the fractions sum to 1.

questions.statement: If MPC = 0.8, then MPS = 0.2.
questions.payload: TRUE. Since MPC + MPS = 1, an MPC of 0.8 implies MPS must be 0.2.

questions.statement: APC + APS = 1 is always true by definition.
questions.payload: TRUE. Total income is either consumed or saved, so APC + APS = 1 at every income level.

questions.statement: The consumption function is written as C = a + bY, where b represents autonomous consumption.
questions.payload: FALSE. In C = a + bY, a is autonomous consumption and b is the MPC.

questions.statement: Autonomous investment is independent of the level of income or output.
questions.payload: TRUE. Autonomous investment is driven by factors like technology and business confidence, not income.

questions.statement: Aggregate demand slopes upward because higher prices stimulate more production.
questions.payload: FALSE. AD slopes downward due to the wealth effect, interest rate effect, and foreign trade effect.

questions.statement: A rise in government spending shifts the AD curve to the right.
questions.payload: TRUE. Higher government spending increases total demand at every price level.

questions.statement: The short-run aggregate supply curve slopes upward.
questions.payload: TRUE. In the short run, higher prices encourage firms to produce more output.

questions.statement: The long-run aggregate supply curve is horizontal at the full employment output level.
questions.payload: FALSE. Long-run AS is vertical at the full employment level of output.

questions.statement: Higher aggregate demand always leads to higher real output regardless of the economy's starting position.
questions.payload: FALSE. At full employment, higher AD raises only the price level, not real output.

questions.statement: Net National Product equals GNP minus depreciation.
questions.payload: TRUE. NNP = GNP - Depreciation, accounting for the wearing out of capital.
